html:
Verve Group SE, MGIMF

Verve is not a clean story, which is exactly why it is interesting. The market is pricing it like a levered, messy European ad-tech roll-up whose best growth days may already be behind it. The forward thesis is different: 2025 was the transition year, not the normalized year. The platform unification is complete, the demand-side stack has been rebuilt around Jun Group and Captify, the company is preparing a corporate relocation that could make a U.S. listing feasible, and the stock is still valued at roughly 4.7x 2026 midpoint adjusted EBITDA.

The current price reference for MGIMF is tricky because the OTC line is illiquid and can print stale. The economically relevant quote is the primary Stockholm/Frankfurt listing. Verve closed May 15, 2026 at SEK 16.15 in Stockholm, about $1.74 per share using current USD/SEK, implying roughly $350 million of equity value versus 200.1 million shares. The U.S. OTC ticker is the wrapper, not the best live price discovery venue.

The key mispricing is that investors are using 2025 reported results as if they represent steady-state economics. They do not. In the Q4 2025 report, reported revenue grew 34% in Q4, like-for-like revenue grew 10%, adjusted EBITDA was €48.6 million, gross margin expanded to 45% from 37% in Q3, client retention hit 99%, total software clients grew 7% sequentially, and the company processed over 1.1 trillion LTM ad impressions. That does not look like a melting ad network. It looks like a company that absorbed integration pain and emerged with better unit economics.

The ugly parts are real, but mostly explain the opportunity. Full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA was only €134.4 million, barely above 2024, while net income collapsed to €0.7 million. Operating cash flow after working capital was just €49.3 million, because advertisers and agencies often pay around 90 days after invoicing while publishers are paid closer to 45 days. Net debt rose to €445.9 million after Captify and Acardo. This is why the equity is cheap. The market sees leverage, weak reported earnings, revenue-recognition complexity, FX noise, and poor cash conversion.

But the forward setup is materially better than that backward snapshot. Management guided 2026 revenue to €680 million to €730 million and adjusted EBITDA to €145 million to €175 million, even while absorbing about €10 million of added sales investment. The guide explicitly assumes a slower first half and stronger second half as new sales cohorts become productive. First Berlin’s March 2026 model has 2026 revenue of €705 million, adjusted EBITDA of €165 million, and free cash flow of €63 million, rising to €99 million in 2027. At today’s share price, the enterprise value is about €745 million, so the stock is trading at about 4.5x to 5.1x 2026 EBITDA and about 4.0x First Berlin’s 2027 adjusted EBITDA.

That is too low if Verve is becoming what management says it is becoming: a scaled, privacy-first, in-app and CTV ad-tech platform with proprietary data assets. The prior simplistic thesis that Verve’s moat is just owned casual games is stale. The stronger version is broader. Verve has consented zero-party data from Jun Group, search-intent data from Captify, ID-less products like ATOM and Moments.AI, owned-and-operated media signals, and now a unified advertiser product launched in January 2026. The LinkedIn case study cited in the Q4 report showed lower cost per install and activation inside Apple’s SKAdNetwork environment, which matters because Apple’s IDFA changes permanently damaged low-quality targeting.

The more speculative upside comes from AI search and conversational intent. First Berlin’s March note argues that Verve can activate anonymized conversational intent signals from major LLM environments, combining zero-party data, search intent, and AI-chat activity into one targeting engine. That is not proven in numbers yet, but it is exactly the sort of variant perception the market is not paying for. If AI interfaces displace some traditional search behavior, Verve may not be a casualty. It may become a buyer-side data layer for a new form of intent.

The second underappreciated catalyst is structural. Verve’s board is asking shareholders at the June 5, 2026 AGM to approve moving the registered office from Sweden to Ireland. The board says the move would make governance more familiar to international and U.S. investors, allow the company to evaluate USD reporting, and create optionality for a future direct U.S. listing, which it says is not currently feasible under the Swedish legal system. This is not a promised Nasdaq listing, but it is a very loud signal about where management wants the shareholder base to go. A small, OTC-neglected European ad-tech stock with U.S.-denominated revenue, U.S. ad-tech peers, USD reporting, and eventual U.S. listing optionality is a different security than MGIMF looks like today.

Retail context also supports the “ignored” angle. Verve is not crowded in U.S. retail channels. The most visible Reddit discussion I found is an old, lightly upvoted r/stocks post from March 2025 discussing the German/Swedish listing, not a current meme-stock thread. That matters because this is not a retail mania thesis. The social signal is mostly absence: almost nobody in the U.S. small-cap crowd is paying attention.

The risk is that leverage turns a cheap stock into a value trap. If ad budgets roll over, working capital keeps consuming cash, or management returns to acquisition-led empire building, the equity can get hit hard. The CIPA lawsuit over alleged SDK data collection is another real risk. So is governance: Bodhivas controls about 23%, Oaktree owns about 20%, and minority shareholders are along for a ride that depends heavily on capital allocation discipline. The revenue-recognition change also means investors should focus on gross profit, adjusted EBITDA, cash conversion, and leverage, not just headline revenue.

The base case does not need heroics. It needs Verve to hit the middle of 2026 guidance, convert the second-half sales ramp into 2027 EBITDA, and turn working capital from a drag into something closer to neutral. If 2027 adjusted EBITDA reaches €185 million and net debt falls toward €330 million, a 6.0x EBITDA multiple gives equity value around €3.90 per share, or about $4.50. That multiple is still a discount to higher-quality ad-tech peers and still penalizes Verve for leverage, complexity, and European small-cap liquidity.

The bull case is what happens if the business is not merely stabilizing, but inflecting. If Captify, Jun Group, Acardo, Verve for Advertisers, and AI/conversational intent lift EBITDA toward €220 million while free cash flow reduces net debt toward €250 million, a 7.0x multiple produces about €6.45 per share, or roughly $7.50. A formal U.S. listing plan or USD-reporting transition could pull that rerating forward.

The bear case is straightforward: ad demand weakens, H1 investment does not convert into H2 growth, EBITDA slips toward €130 million, working capital remains structurally cash-hungry, and the market values the business at only 4.75x under €465 million of net debt. That produces about $0.85 per share.

Bear target: $0.85, about 51% downside from the May 15 economic current price of about $1.74.

Base target: $4.50, about 159% upside.

Bull target: $7.50, about 331% upside.
